fuzzy_prd:
We need a reusable payload sanitizer that can scrub sensitive data from API request and response payloads before they are forwarded to any monitoring or logging destination. The primary goal is to ensure no secrets such as passwords, card numbers, identity numbers, or access credentials are ever transmitted in plain text to third-party observability tools.

The sanitizer accepts a flat map of field names to their values. For each field whose name is recognized as sensitive, the value should be replaced with a masking character repeated as many times as the length of the original value — preserving the shape of the data while destroying the content. If a sensitive field holds no value (i.e., it is empty/absent), it should be passed through as-is without conversion. All non-sensitive fields must be returned completely unchanged, and the original ordering of all fields must be preserved in the output.

The set of field names considered sensitive should have sensible defaults built in, covering the most common cases teams typically encounter. That default set should be configurable so teams can extend or override it without modifying the core logic — handle the configuration loading the same way the service provider module handles its config merging.

The output of the sanitizer must be a compact, single-line JSON object written to standard output, followed by a newline. Error conditions such as invalid input or unrecognized actions should emit a short diagnostic line rather than crashing.

The masking logic must be physically and logically decoupled from the I/O adapter layer, so the core business rules can be tested in isolation. The whole system should be structured appropriately for its complexity — neither over-engineered nor bundled into a single monolithic file.

A couple extra specifics from the questions that came back: the built-in masked field list is exactly ['password', 'pwd', 'secret', 'password_confirmation', 'cc', 'card_number', 'ccv', 'ssn', 'credit_score', 'api_key']. That same list lives in config/treblle.php under 'masked_fields' and also exists as a hardcoded fallback inside the maskFields method itself, so we still have the expected defaults even if config is not giving us anything.

On matching, field names are checked with preg_match('/\b' . $field . '\b/mi', (string) $key). So this is case-insensitive, uses whole-word boundaries, and avoids partial substring matches. On the value side, if a sensitive field is null, it stays null and does not get turned into a string or masked. The check is $data[$key] = $value != null ? str_repeat('*', strlen($value)) : $value; so null just passes through unchanged. If it is a sensitive non-null string, we replace it with '*' repeated strlen($value) times, using the original byte-length. Example: 'hunter2' becomes '*******'.

Also, the dispatcher output is literally json_encode($masked) and then "\n" written to STDOUT, so the result is compact one-line JSON with no extra whitespace. Example output looks like {"cc":null,"otherValue":"something","password":"****"}\n

trigger_keywords: what happens when input is not an array
return value when data is not an array
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If $data is not an array, maskFields returns an empty array []. The guard at the top of the method: if (!is_array($data)) { return []; } handles this case.

trigger_keywords: how are nested array values handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a field value is itself an array, maskFields calls itself recursively on that nested array. However, the result of the recursive call is NOT assigned back to $data[$key], meaning nested arrays are traversed but the masked nested values are discarded — nested fields are effectively not masked in the output.

trigger_keywords: which environments are ignored by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default ignored environments (where Treblle does not send data) are 'dev' and 'test', configured as: env('TREBLLE_IGNORED_ENV', 'dev,test') in config/treblle.php. This is a comma-separated string that is split with explode(',', ...) during the check.

trigger_keywords: what action does the dispatcher accept
valid action values in JSON request
what happens when action is unknown
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher only accepts action='mask'. If the action field is missing, it defaults to 'mask'. If action is anything other than 'mask', the dispatcher writes 'error=unknown_action\naction=<value>\n' to STDOUT and exits with code 0.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle the configuration loading the same way the service provider module handles its config merging
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The TreblleServiceProvider (golden/src/TreblleServiceProvider.php) uses $this->mergeConfigFrom(__DIR__.'/../config/treblle.php', 'treblle') in its register() method. The maskFields method similarly provides a fallback default array as the second argument to config('treblle.masked_fields', [...]) so that if config is not published the defaults still apply.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question does not match any specific constraint in the oracle. Please consult the source implementation in golden/src/Middlewares/TreblleMiddleware.php and the configuration file golden/config/treblle.php for authoritative details.

feature1_sensitive_field_masking.json.description: Redact sensitive values from a flat request payload (a map of field name to value). Any field whose name matches the configured set of sensitive field names has its value replaced by a run of asterisks whose length equals the length of the original value's string form; a sensitive field whose value is null is left as null (nothing to redact); fields whose names are not in the sensitive set are passed through unchanged. Key order is preserved. The output is the resulting payload serialized as a JSON object.
